Think about that for a second. A very common, restricting, and self-defeating idea is that we get upset because bad things hap- pen—that happiness or unhappiness is externally caused and that we cannot do anything about it. This is not true. Happiness and unhappiness are internally caused, and we can freely choose either one. Your joy and happiness are not dictated by your experiences in life, but by your relationship to those expe- riences. What you’ll learn in this book is that   events   don’t make you upset—our stress, emotional disturbance and negative feel- ings are all caused by our own internal mental contamination!  The Negative Feedback Superhighway  There are three reasons why you are the way you are right now. Number one is genetic predisposition. We are predisposed to think, feel, and react in certain ways. Millions of years of nat- ural selection led to your parents creating you, and with that you became predisposed to all sorts of tendencies like Anxiety, Depression and so on.
pages.15: Mental Contamination  10 Number two is environmental conditioning. You   can   change the environmental conditions in your present life situation, and that may be a good idea, but to be able to do that you must recognize the third and most important reason why you are the way you are right now. Number three is self-conditioning; that is, the way you talk to yourself, the story in your head. The Buddhists call it the mon- key mind. I call it The Narrator or sometimes the 95 Maniacs in your head! No matter what you call it, it represents the 60,000+ sentences that we say in our mind every day. This running monologue we generate is why we are tired at the end of the day. The way we describe ourselves to ourselves, the stories we construct, inform our opinions of ourselves and the world around us. Some say that the mind is the neglected organ. In our society, we go to the dentist to have perfect teeth, we go to the gym to have a perfect body, but very few people take the time to de- velop a strong, clear mind. The vast amount of these sentences come directly from cognitive habits—thought patterns we’re so used to we don’t even question whether they’re true or not. These patterns can play out in a negative feedback loop, a never-ending cycle of distressing thoughts, feelings, behaviors, and physiological effects. Our self-created conceptions inspire painful emotions that lead to undesirable actions. We then create more conceptions about our negative actions and those actions get our adrenaline pumping. Now we’re thinking more negative thoughts and the loop begins again. The effects of your inner dialogue on your emotions and behavior are almost immeasurable! If you do not learn how to observe this constant dialogue, you can’t change it or the habits it creates. It’s like driving in circles on a highway—you’re dizzy and running out of gas with no relief in sight! The Narrator runs the inner dialogue, and the Observer can observe and change the inner dialogue. The Narrator is the part of the mind that makes up the ‘ego.’ It is what we think of and identify as our ‘self,’ the author of all we think, feel, and believe. It takes the input you receive through your senses—by itself only impartial bits of abstract information—and assigns meaning based on our beliefs, feel- ings, and conceptions. It tells us stories that make up our men- tal formations of who we are and how we relate to the world around us. These stories may be in line with provable facts, but because they’re happening in our head where no one else can check, they may not be. The danger of conception is that it
pages.19: Mental Contamination  14 need not have any relationship with fact at all. It can devolve into self-delusion without warning and often without any conscious thought. How do we shift to the Observer? In order to become or culti- vate the Observer, you must shift from being your thoughts to noticing your thoughts—that is, being consciously aware of— your thoughts. You act as a spectator without getting wrapped up in or attached to the thought process. It’s as if you are watch- ing a movie. Just about everyone procrastinates at one time or another. It is a very human thing to do, and there is no need to put yourself down when you become aware of having procrastinated.  Identifying Procrastination  Identifying why you procrastinate is an important step towards getting your tasks done. Once you know how you tend to block your efforts, you can actively work to overcome procrastina- tion. Here are some reasons for procrastination:
pages.139: The Formula for Guilt  134 Fear of the Future: You are afraid of failing. If you pushed ahead and completed the task, it may not turn out as well as you thought it would. As long as you don’t attempt to do a task, you don’t have the possibility of failure. Awfulizing: You exaggerate the consequences of a less-than- perfect outcome. You tell yourself it would be awful if you failed and that you couldn’t stand it, so you put it off. Unrealistic Expectations: You think you can do much more  WKDQ\RXFDQ<RXSLOHXSWDVNVDQGWKHQȴQG\RXUVHOIXQ - able to complete them. Eventually you feel overwhelmed and put off doing anything at all. Approval: You’re afraid to say no. You are afraid you may lose other people’s approval which you think you need in order to approve of yourself.  Understanding Procrastination  Now that you have some reasons for procrastination, here are some of the ways we keep procrastinating tendencies alive: Rationalizing: You tell yourself things that in your mind justify the delay: “I’ll start tomorrow,” “I’m tired and as soon as I’ve rested, I’ll start,” or the very popular, “I’ve admitted I’m a procrastinator now, so I can play the role honestly,” and then, last but not least, “I always do my best work at the eleventh hour.” More B=S. Impulsivity: You give up because the task seems too hard. Then, from time to time, you burst with energy and work hard for the quick kill, which doesn’t come. You then prove to yourself that the world is too hard for you and you give up.
pages.140: The Formula for Guilt  135 Escapism: Instead of doing what needs to be done, you believe a magical solution will present itself if you just wait and are patient. Instead of working hard for success, you substitute fantasy for reality which will bring you only tem- porary relief. You might escape into television, books, walks, and minor tasks that will keep your mind off the things you need to do in order to succeed.  Overcoming Procrastination  Recognize that it is what you are telling yourself that de- feats your efforts. Begin now. Stop believing you need inspiration before you start. Getting into a task usually brings inspiration. If you avoid the task, inspiration may never come.  $FFHSWWKDW\RXDUHKXPDQDQGZLOOȴQGLWGLɘFXOWWRDF - complish hard tasks. Write yourself reminders and place them where you will see them every day. Remind yourself not to procrastinate and you will help yourself achieve your goals. Know that you can change. Sometimes you will backslide and while it is normal, it is not permanent. When you see yourself procrastinating do not conclude that you are beyond help and will never change. pages.168: Lessons From the 10 Ox-Herding Pictures  163 INTRODUCTION Many years ago I went on a retreat with Father Robert Kennedy who is a Catholic priest and a Zen Buddhist teacher and the au- thor of several books on Buddhism. He studied with both Bernie Glassman Roshi and Taizan Maezumi Roshi two wonderful Zen teachers and mentors. I was so enthralled with his teaching during our weekend Sesshin that I bought all of his books. His book Zen Gifts to Christians is based on the famous Ten Ox-herding pictures of Zen. It is a unique way to review and understand some of the basic principles of Zen in a fun and inspiring way. John M. Koller in the Department of Cognitive Science at Renssaelaer Polytechnic Institute has written a wonderful pa- per on it as well entitled: Ox-Herding: Stages of Zen Practice. He wrote this in his introduction: The ten ox-herding pictures and commentaries presented here depict the stages of practice leading to the enlightenment at which Zen (Chan) Buddhism aims. They dramatize the fact that enlightenment reveals the true self, showing it to be the ordi- nary self doing ordinary things in the most extraordinary way. Wonderful! I just love that idea that Buddhism helps us discover our true self in its ordinariness and its extraordinariness! 1     7HQVKď6KĭEXQ6KRNRNXML7HPSOHZHEVLWH3XEOLF'RPDLQKWWSVFRPPRQVZLNL - media.org/w/index.php?curid=2350512

This can be interpreted to mean that the separation of practice and realization has been over- come, as has the separation of ordinary reality and the ultimate reality. Until now he has been practicing meditation as a means of achieving enlightenment. But with realization of the non-du- ality of existence comes awareness of the identity of means and ends; practice itself is realization (page 6). 13 This journey of ours may seem fruitless and endless at times. Remember it took time and patience and forethought for the lonely ox-herder to discover that the bliss of enlightenment was not something to be searching for as if it was lost some- 13 Koller, J.M. http://www.columbia.edu/cu/weai/exeas/resources/pdf/oxherding.pdf
pages.185: Lessons From the 10 Ox-Herding Pictures  180 where on the trail of his journey, but something that was always there within him. If you have a practice already take a little time to think about what it is, where it is, and how it is affecting your daily life. Our practice is not to be something or someone that is different from our life. It is not something that we set aside time for in the morning or evening to sit on our cushion and meditate to “become” enlightened. Our practice is not separate from our life it is our life. It is every word we speak, every move we make, every thought we think. It is who we are. As Koller says, “…practice itself is realization.” Realization is our practice in motion all day every day. Thus, we can live a more serene life, we can rest in the assurance that we don’t have to be anything or go anywhere to transcend this earthly life and its trials and tribulations. With non-duality, there is only this moment, this word, this breath, this experience. It is how we deal with “just this” moment that makes us feel or seem that in this life we will never see enlightenment. Just “THIS,” as we say in Buddhism, is enlightenment. So, don’t rush to that mountain top, or forest, or valley hide away in search of it. It is already here with you, within you, and around you. Take a deep breath each time you realize it and awaken to your ultimate reality: the existence of non-duality means that you are one with all there is. Now go out and live it!

tree.title: Four Blocks Paperback Book
tree.summary: Comprehensive guide to emotional wellness using the Four Blocks methodology.

nodes.title: Preface
nodes.summary: In the preface, the author reflects on their extensive experience with two significant mentors: Dr. Albert Ellis, who developed Rational Emotive Behavior Therapy (REBT), and Steve Hagen, a Zen Priest, who enriched the application of these concepts. The author emphasizes that despite the myriad of problems people face, most emotional struggles boil down to four key obstacles—Anger, Anxiety, Depression, and Guilt—that hinder personal happiness and contentment.

nodes.title: Introduction
nodes.summary: the beliefs we hold can deeply affect our emotional well-being and perception of reality. This chapter introduces the concept that suffering stems from four primary emotions—Anger, Anxiety, Depression, and Guilt—and presents a guide for transforming these negative states into positive qualities referred to as The Four Immeasurables: lovingkindness, compassion, empathetic joy, and equanimity, drawing on ancient wisdom to help alleviate ignorance and promote lasting happiness.

nodes.title: Mental Contamination
nodes.summary: Chapter 1 of "Mental Contamination" explores the notion that our emotional states are not solely determined by external circumstances but are primarily shaped by our internal mental processes and self-talk, which often harbor negativity and distortion. The author emphasizes the critical role of self-conditioning, highlighting how detrimental beliefs that go unchallenged can lead to mental contamination and a misperception of reality, ultimately hindering personal growth and emotional well-being.

nodes.title: The Three Insights To A Mind Of Peace
nodes.summary: Chapter 2 explores the concept that all emotional disturbances stem from four primary negative emotions: Anger, Anxiety, Depression, and Guilt, and emphasizes that true happiness comes from within through the understanding and management of one's own thoughts, feelings, and behaviors. The chapter introduces the Three Insights to a Mind of Peace, the first being that individuals are 100% responsible for their own emotional states, and that reliance on external factors for happiness ultimately leads to blame and unhappiness.

nodes.title: The ABCs of How Emotions are Created
nodes.summary: This chapter emphasizes that emotions are influenced by our beliefs, distinguishing between rational beliefs—accepting reality as it is—and irrational beliefs, which resist reality and lead to negative emotions. Acceptance of all experiences, whether positive or negative, is vital for happiness, as resisting reality creates cognitive dissonance and suffering.

nodes.title: The Seven Irrational Beliefs
nodes.summary: Chapter 4 discusses the concept of seven irrational beliefs that contribute to human suffering and emotional distress, emphasizing that our thoughts can lead to negative physiological reactions such as anger, anxiety, and depression. By recognizing and challenging these irrational beliefs, individuals can gain control over their emotions and foster a healthier mindset, ultimately leading to greater peace and contentment.

nodes.title: The Formula for Anger
nodes.summary: In "The Formula for Anger," Vince Parr explains that anger is a universal response triggered by egocentric thinking combined with expectations of what should or must happen (A = ET + S). He emphasizes that our belief that the world should cater to our desires leads to frustration, noting that understanding our relative insignificance in the vast universe can help mitigate unwarranted anger.

nodes.title: The Formula for Anxiety
nodes.summary: In this chapter, the author explains that anxiety is a future-oriented emotion stemming from a specific thought process encapsulated in the formula AX = WI + AW + ICSI, which stands for "What If?" + "Awfulizing" + "I Can't Stand It!" Each component represents a way of thinking that exaggerates fears about potential negative outcomes, ultimately undermining one's confidence and capabilities. To mitigate anxiety, the author suggests confronting these thought patterns, addressing the situations that trigger anxiety, and allowing oneself to move forward despite fears.

nodes.title: The Formula for Depression
nodes.summary: Chapter 7 discusses the painful nature of depression and outlines a mathematical formula for understanding it, consisting of three main components: hopelessness, helplessness, and need. Hopelessness includes negative outlooks toward the past, present, and future, while helplessness encompasses self-pity, pity for others, and self-rejection, ultimately illustrating how these thought processes contribute to depressive feelings.

nodes.title: The Formula for Guilt
nodes.summary: In Chapter 8, "The Formula for Guilt," Vince Parr discusses how guilt stems from beliefs of wrongness and worthlessness, emphasizing that feelings of guilt arise not from the actual events we experience but from the "should" statements we attach to them. He explains that these irrational beliefs distort our self-perception and contribute to a cycle of guilt, reinforcing the idea that we are inherently flawed and unworthy, when in fact, all humans are fallible.

nodes.title: The Formulas for Happiness
nodes.summary: In this chapter, the author emphasizes the importance of unconditional self-acceptance, arguing that individuals should not equate their self-worth with achievements or external validation, but rather strive for a fulfilling life that minimizes pain and maximizes enjoyment. Additionally, it addresses procrastination, identifying common underlying fears such as fear of failure, unrealistic expectations, and the need for approval, and highlights the necessity of understanding these reasons to overcome procrastination effectively.

nodes.title: Why Zen Meditation is Essential Mind Training
nodes.summary: In this chapter, the author emphasizes the importance of accepting oneself and others unconditionally, recognizing that everyone is fallible and will make mistakes, which contributes to overall happiness. It also advocates for staying present in the moment and avoiding the distractions of regret or anxiety about the past and future, suggesting that mindfulness and acceptance lead to a more fulfilling and less disturbed life.

nodes.title: Healthy Body, Healthy Mind
nodes.summary: The chapter emphasizes that understanding the foundations of rational thinking is crucial for applying the formulas for happiness effectively, as unpleasant events are inevitable and can disrupt one's happiness if not managed properly. It advocates for Zen meditation as a vital practice to enhance mind awareness, helping individuals observe their thoughts and feelings without attachment, thereby enabling them to handle disturbances and cultivate a state of happiness.

nodes.title: Lessons From the 10 Ox-Herding Pictures
nodes.summary: The chapter emphasizes the importance of improving digestive health and overall wellbeing through quality nutrition and adequate hydration. It highlights the benefits of caloric restriction and intermittent fasting for mental and physical health while underscoring the necessity of drinking sufficient water, as dehydration can significantly impair cognitive functions and overall health.

nodes.title: Epilogue
nodes.summary: This chapter emphasizes the simplicity and mindfulness of everyday life, illustrating that true enlightenment is found in the act of "chopping wood and carrying water," where one can engage fully and compassionately with the world. It highlights the significance of using our hands and actions to bless others and cultivate a creative and loving presence, as demonstrated by the enlightened ox-herd who embodies joy in the ordinary tasks he performs within society.

nodes.title: Acknowledgements
nodes.summary: In the acknowledgments chapter, the author expresses deep gratitude to a wide array of influential thinkers, philosophers, and educators who shaped their understanding and teachings, particularly in the realm of rational living. They specifically thank their friend Neil Schauer for encouraging the development of the book and online course, as well as Melissa Gridley and Statler Gause for their invaluable editing and production efforts, all aimed at helping others overcome challenges related to irrational thinking and emotional pain.

nodes.title: The Four Blocks to Happiness Mastery Course
nodes.summary: This chapter introduces "The Four Blocks to Happiness Mastery Course," a comprehensive online program developed by The Parr Institute for Mental Wellness, designed as a companion to the book. It includes various instructional materials such as videos, audio lessons, exercises, and worksheets, all aimed at enhancing the understanding of happiness mastery concepts presented in the book.
